title: From 88 to 3,432: Building Complete Visual Regression Testing in 5 Days
summary: A frontend project with 600+ components had only 8% visual regression coverage. After 5 days, 805M tokens, and 153 subagents later, coverage hit 55%. The lessons from failures turned out more valuable than the code itself.
body:
## The First Screenshot Was Blank

It all started with a blank screenshot.

I had already written the Playwright spec, mocked 30+ API endpoints, injected auth state into localStorage, and was confident that `toHaveScreenshot()` would give me a beautiful dashboard screenshot. When I opened the PNG—pure white.

The terrifying part: **the test passed.** Playwright's `toHaveScreenshot()` doesn't care if your screenshot is blank or shows a complete page—as long as both screenshots match, it passes. Your baseline repository could be full of white images, and CI would still be green.

This was one of the most important discoveries of the entire session, but we'll get to that later. First, let me explain why we did this.

---

## 8% Coverage, 92% Blind Spots

Mitsein is an AI agent platform with roughly 600 `.tsx` files in the frontend. We already had a component catalog (`/debug/mui-test`) with 88 visual regression baselines in Playwright—but this only covered isolated components in the catalog, the "showroom" stuff.

What about the real production UI?

| Category | Total | Catalog Coverage | Blind Spots |
|----------|-------|------------------|-------------|
| Pages/Routes | 42 | 1 | 41 |
| Dialogs/Modals | ~50 | ~1 | ~49 |
| Settings Tabs | 15 | 0 | 15 |
| Thread Components | 73 | ~5 | ~68 |
| Admin Panels | 12 | 0 | 12 |

49 dialogs—the most interaction-heavy parts of the user experience—had **zero visual regression coverage**. Settings panels, billing flows, admin dashboards, all at zero.

This wasn't a "we need more tests" problem. This was a "we have no defense whatsoever" problem. Anyone could change a theme token and silently break the styling of 40 dialogs, and nobody would know.

---

## Route Mocking: The Only Leverage Point

The core challenge: these pages all need authenticated state, API data, and complete React provider chain initialization. Directly opening `/dashboard` redirects to login, opening `/settings` shows a loading spinner then white screen.

The initial attempt was injecting a stress token into cookies, letting Playwright access like a real user. This required the backend running on port 8000. Problem: the backend wasn't under my control, and E2E test flakiness would destroy the credibility of the entire visual regression suite.

**The real leverage point was Playwright's route interception.**

```typescript
await page.route(
  /\/api\/(?!.*\.(ts|tsx|js|jsx|mjs|css|json|svg|png))/,
  async (route) => {
    const url = route.request().url();
    if (url.includes('/api/auth/me')) {
      return route.fulfill(json(mock.AUTH_ME));
    }
    // ... 40+ more endpoints
  }
);
```

That regex negative lookahead was born from desperation. The first version used a `**/*api*` glob pattern, which intercepted Vite's ESM module imports (`/src/lib/api/utils.ts` contains `api`), returning JSON to a browser that expected a JavaScript module—the entire SPA crashed.

It took a full debug cycle to discover this issue. The lesson: route mocking patterns must precisely match only HTTP API calls, never touch bundler module resolution.

---

## The Provider Chain Wall

Even with route mocking solved, there was still a wall: React's provider chain.

Mitsein's SPA has a deeply nested provider tree—AuthProvider → QueryClientProvider → ThemeProvider → RouterProvider → ... If the auth check returns 401 but the shape isn't exactly right, some provider bails out, and the entire `#root` innerHTML becomes an empty string.

I tried many combinations—mocking different response shapes, injecting different localStorage keys. Some pages would render, others stayed white forever. The final discovery: **pages like dashboard and settings that deeply depend on the provider chain require mocking the complete API surface to work.**

`mockAllAPIs()` eventually covered 40+ endpoints: auth/me, billing/can-run, thread/list, agent-store/catalog, project/list, favorites, cron, memory, workflow, connectors... Miss any one, and some provider might suspend or error, causing white screen.

This is where the `setupAuthenticatedPage()` helper came from—it injects theme, auth state, and all API mocks in one go, ensuring the provider chain can fully initialize.

---

## 3,432 Screenshots, 0 Blank

The final spec architecture splits by UI area, not functionality:

| Spec | Content | Baselines |
|------|---------|----------|
| route-coverage | 42 navigable routes | 336 |
| theme-matrix | 35+ catalog tabs | 864 |
| settings-tabs + dialog-settings-extra | 9 tabs + 6 sub-tabs | 432 |
| dialog-* (10 specs) | 26+ dialogs | ~1,200 |
| empty-states | 7 empty state pages | 168 |
| auth-public-pages | login/signup/tos/privacy/share/updates | 144 |
| thread-components | 3 thread variants | 72 |

Each test case runs 4 themes × 2 modes × 3 viewports = 24 screenshots. 18 spec files totaling 3,432 baselines.

Why split by area instead of functionality? A "billing" feature spans settings pages, dialogs, routes, and empty states. Splitting by functionality means each spec maintains its own mock configuration. Splitting by area means each spec only cares about one trigger pattern (URL navigation vs button click vs keyboard shortcut), making mock configuration more cohesive.

Full regeneration took over 6 hours (18 specs × ~15 minutes/spec). Used background tasks + CronCreate with 15-minute polling monitoring, not blocking the context window. This pattern is worth remembering: long processes use fire-and-forget execution + lightweight polling, don't try to tail -f a 6-hour process.

---

## Blank Screenshot Audit: Testing the Tests

Back to the original problem: `toHaveScreenshot()` doesn't care if screenshot content is meaningful.

The solution is file size auditing: blank PNGs are usually < 6KB, normal UI pages are 50-200KB. After each regeneration, run:

```bash
find __screenshots__ -name '*.png' -size -6k
```

If output is non-empty, there are blank baselines. This must be a CI gate, not manual checking. Otherwise your 3,432 baselines might hide dozens of white images—tests all green, coverage all fake.

This is a meta-verification pattern: **your testing tool itself needs a test.** `toHaveScreenshot()` verifies visual consistency but not visual correctness. File size audit fills this gap.

---

## Honest 5-Day Assessment

Numbers: 805M tokens, 5,711 LLM calls, 5,601 tool calls, 153 subagents, estimated cost $1,379.

Results: From 88 baselines to 3,432 baselines, coverage from 8% to roughly 55%. 18 spec files + complete mock infrastructure + playbook HTML report (143 pages, 18 categories).

Imperfections:

1. **Still ~17 uncovered dialogs**. These require complex multi-step UI interactions to trigger (like downgrade-offer needs billing → click downgrade → confirm), reliability isn't high enough.

2. **Insufficient thread sub-component coverage**. Originally planned 50 variants, actually did 3. Presentation decks, Excel viewers, Word doc renderers need more complex mock data (file artifacts, PPTX parsing results), ROI not compelling.

3. **Large estimation variance**. Early estimate was 4,560 baselines, final was 3,432. Reason: overestimated thread-components variant count (50 → 3). Estimates should be based on actual spec analysis, not multiplication fantasies.

4. **Context window pressure**. 5-day continuous session caused multiple context compactions, each losing some intermediate state. Long session trade-off: continuity vs context freshness. Not sure if 5-day sessions are optimal, maybe 2-3 day cycles would be better.

---

## Things Worth Taking Away

**Route mocking is the leverage point for visual testing.** Invest once in building `mockAllAPIs()`, every new page/dialog test reuses it directly. Mock at the HTTP boundary, not component level—preserves complete theme, layout, routing render chains.

**Blank screenshots are silent failures.** `toHaveScreenshot()` won't fail because screenshots are blank. File size audit must be a CI gate. This is a variant of tautological testing—tests that appear to verify but actually verify nothing.

**Split specs by UI area, not functionality.** Dialog specs split by trigger type (keyboard, billing, thread), route specs split by page. Functional splitting creates cross-cutting mock dependencies.

**Long processes use background + cron, not pipes.** Piping Playwright stdout causes SIGPIPE to kill worker processes. Redirect to files, poll files with cron. This debug cost me a full cycle, but once known, never stepped on again.

---

## Next Steps

3,432 baselines now sit in git (commit `2323c128b`), but no CI integration yet. This means they're currently "there" but not "in use"—anyone can break UI then push, baselines won't auto-diff.

Next step is adding visual regression to CI pipeline, with `--workers=1` (shared Vite server constraint) and blank audit gates. Plus those 17 remaining dialog coverage—though it's diminishing returns territory, interaction-heavy dialogs justify the investment.

55% coverage isn't the endpoint, but it's more than an order of magnitude better than 8%. The remaining 45% is mostly thread sub-components (presentation decks, file renderers)—these need more refined mock data design, that's next session's work.
